howTo.name: How to convert JPG to WebP online
howTo.description: Step-by-step guide to converting JPG files to WebP.

steps.name: Add JPG files
steps.text: Drag JPG files onto the drop zone or click to browse. Multiple files supported.

steps.name: Set WebP quality
steps.text: Adjust the quality slider (60–95%). Default 80% balances size and quality.

steps.name: Download WebP files
steps.text: Download files individually or all at once. File sizes shown for comparison.

contentBlocks.title: Why convert JPG to WebP?
contentBlocks.html:
WebP is a modern image format developed by Google that delivers significantly better compression than JPG. At equivalent visual quality, WebP files are typically 25–35% smaller than their JPG counterparts.

Smaller images mean faster page loads, lower bandwidth costs, and better Core Web Vitals scores. WebP is supported by all major browsers including Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge.

This tool requires a desktop browser because WebP encoding relies on the Canvas API with WebP export support. All processing happens locally.

contentBlocks.title: How to convert JPG to WebP

items.title: 1. Add JPG files
items.description: Drag your JPG images onto the upload area or click to select them. No limit on files.

items.title: 2. Set quality
items.description: Choose the WebP quality level. Lower value = smaller file, higher = sharper image.

items.title: 3. Download WebP
items.description: Download each file or grab them all at once. Size comparison shown for every file.

contentBlocks.title: Quality settings — what to choose when converting JPG to WebP?
contentBlocks.html:
The quality slider lets you set a value from 60% to 95%. Higher values mean better image quality but larger file sizes.

  • 80% (default) — a good balance for most use cases: business websites, blogs, articles. The visual difference compared to the original is virtually unnoticeable.
  • 85–90% — for product photography, portfolios, and galleries where quality is the priority.
  • 60–70% — when minimizing file size is the priority, e.g. thumbnails in category grids.

At 80% quality, the visual difference between the original JPG and the resulting WebP is virtually imperceptible to the naked eye, while the file is significantly smaller.

contentBlocks.title: How image conversion impacts page speed and SEO
contentBlocks.html:
Core Web Vitals is a set of performance metrics Google uses when evaluating websites. One of them — LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) — measures the time it takes for the largest visible element to appear on screen. On many pages, that element is an image.

Converting JPG images to WebP reduces graphic file sizes, which directly shortens resource download time and improves the LCP score. Smaller files mean faster page loading — especially important on mobile devices with slower connections. Additional techniques like loading="lazy" and fetchpriority="high" speed up rendering.

Tools like PageSpeed Insights and Lighthouse identify specific files worth optimizing.

contentBlocks.title: Tips for converting JPG to WebP
contentBlocks.description: A few tips to help you avoid common issues during conversion:

items.title: Very large files
items.description: Converting images above 4000×4000 pixels may be slower and strain the browser. If processing many large files, consider splitting them into batches of 10–20.

items.title: Already compressed files
items.description: If the original JPG file was already heavily compressed, converting to WebP may not yield significant size savings. Try different quality settings.

items.title: Transparency
items.description: The WebP format supports alpha channel (transparency). If the original JPG file contains transparent areas, they will be preserved.

items.title: Keep your originals
items.description: Lossy conversion is irreversible. Keep copies of original JPG files so you can re-convert with different settings if needed.

contentBlocks.title: How much can you save by converting JPG to WebP?
contentBlocks.html:
Savings depend on the source file type and its original compression. Below are example results:

Camera photo

2.4 MB → 890 KB

Saving: ~63%

Product image

500 KB → 185 KB

Saving: ~63%

Screenshot / banner

350 KB → 230 KB

Saving: ~34%

Actual savings may vary depending on image content and quality settings. The converter shows the exact size before and after conversion for each file.

contentBlocks.title: Frequently asked questions about JPG to WebP conversion

items.question: How much smaller will the WebP be?
items.answer: Typically 25–35% smaller than JPG at equivalent visual quality.

items.question: Is quality lost?
items.answer: WebP uses lossy compression like JPG. At 80% quality the difference is virtually invisible.

items.question: Do all browsers support WebP?
items.answer: Yes. Chrome, Firefox, Safari (14.6+), Edge, and Opera all support WebP.

items.question: Why is this desktop-only?
items.answer: WebP encoding via Canvas API requires a desktop browser. Mobile support varies.

items.question: Are files uploaded to a server?
items.answer: No. Everything runs locally in your browser.

items.question: Can I convert WebP back to JPG?
items.answer: Yes. Use our WebP to JPG converter.

items.question: What is the maximum file size?
items.answer: Individual files up to 100 MB are supported.
